turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Number 67, F.L. Shuttlesworth et al., Petitioner, versus City of Birmingham. I beg your pardon. This is Number 66, James Gober et al. versus City of Birmingham. Mrs. Motley.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice and may it please the Court. The petitioners in this case are 10 Negro students attending Daniel Payne College in Birmingham, Alabama. They are here pursuant to Title 28, United States Code Section 1257, seeking a reversal of their convictions for trespass after warning by the Alabama Court. Now, relying on the Due Process and Equal Protection Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment, these petitioners repeatedly find the law and assert here that their convictions are constitutionally void because they are the product of state action manifestly prohibited by the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. Now all 10 petitioners were arrested on March 31st, 1960 following sit-in demonstrations in five Birmingham department stores. They at that time were seeking food service at a lunch counter limited to white persons. Each petitioner was charged and convicted of violating Section 1436 of the Birmingham Code, a trespass after warning statute which appears on page 2 of our brief. There are five trials in these cases, one, petitioner was tried and then it was stipulated that the testimony as to that one petitioner would apply to his companion. You see, two petitioners went into each of these five stores. And they were first tried in the Recorder's Court of the City of Birmingham and convicted and then appealed to the Circuit Court, their convictions were -- they affirmed and affirmed by the Court of Appeals of Alabama which is the only court that wrote an opinion and certiorari in the Supreme Court of the State was denied. Now the significant facts in all of these cases are relatively the same. The first important fact is that Birmingham has a city ordinance which makes it unlawful to serve Negroes and whites in the same room. That ordinance appears on page 2 of our brief. Now this ordinance is a part of a massive state policy of racial segregation, is set forth in our brief the Alabama segregation statutes. All of the petitioners of course are Negroes. For one successfully sought service in department stores where Negroes are welcomed and served at all counters except the lunch counter where this ordinance requires that there be racial segregation. Now, in every one of these cases, the petitioners were asked to leave the counter by some representative of the store in the sense that they were told that they could not be served there or we have a counter for Negroes on the fourth floor, in the basement once you go there. Or in some instances, the counter was closed after the Negroes appeared and were told this and didn't leave. Now, the police arrived and the police arrived in some instances when the people were still eating and the police arrived and not pursuant to a call from any member of the store in any case. And this -- that the record is absolutely clear on that that no employee in any one of the five stores called the police. Moreover --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Does the record show how the police got there? Why they --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- why they came there?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes. And every case, the officer testified that he was directed to go there either by a superior officer or by a call over the radio in his car from police headquarters.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: It doesn't show the chain behind that.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: No, it doesn't. But he testified that he was directed by a superior or a call from the police headquarters and every store employee testified that he or she did not call the police --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: There's no --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- and that was clear.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: No indication in any of the cases that the police were already there when (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: No sir, I don't believe there's any indication to that effect.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Your case differs from the other then and that as I understand it, if the restaurant owner could not have chosen to serve --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, I would say --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- without making himself a violator of the city law?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Oh, I would say in this case like -- unlike the others, we have the ordinance which was clearly in the record I think or the Court certainly was required to take judicial notice of it but I want to make it clear that even if they were not an ordinance in this case, the rest of the petitioners and their conviction in this matter in these circumstances would still violate the Equal Protection Clause if this --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understand that's your argument but --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- certainly in this case, you have a city law which he would've violate -- would've violated had they served this.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And they could not make a choice of their own free will but that's if -- if there'd been the other way without subjecting in there the prosecution and conviction for --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- of this ordinance.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible) of law by the -- (Inaudible) that's the constitution?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes sir, on page 24 of the record, the representative from Kress' Department Store was on the stand and he was asked what the petitioners have been told. Answer, on page 24, he told them, “It would be against the law to serve them there”. Then petitioners counsel asked him about this ordinance which I've just referred to and there was an objection by the respondent -- by the lawyer for the city and the judge sustained that, he said he didn't think the witness -- his knowledge of the law was material or anything like that and the counsel for the defendants here said, “This is the whole theory of our case”. And the Court would not permit him to develop that theory of the case. He said that, “We want to show”, as he says at the bottom, “It is our theory of this case, it is one, they simply on the city segregation ordinance”.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Oh, I assume the -- I assume that such an ordinance would be unconstitutional.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Oh, certainly.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And a -- but and a -- is there anything -- is there -- there is some evidence in the record that this wasn't a dead letter of ordinance, it was just a -- hadn't been cleared from the books (Inaudible) but that it really had some teeth in it that it was a meaningful ordinance in this context at this time?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, as I say that the Court was not -- have been to develop it but I think this case shows -- this record shows that this ordinance was what was being enforced because each police officer came in to the store and on the stand he was asked, “What did you see unusual as you came in to the store?” And each one replied, “I saw two Negroes sitting on the couch”, and for him that was enough.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Isn't -- this didn't have specific reference to the ordinance now --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: No, he didn't refer to -- specifically to an ordinance but in one case, he said, “I arrested them under the authority of the City of Birmingham”. But the police officer, he -- in most instances he spoke to no one as he came in. In one case he said, “Mrs. Evans gave me the complaint”. The petitioner said, they never saw Mrs. Evans until that -- she got to court, the Recorder's Court so that the testimony in each case says that the police officers went in and did not speak to any employee of the store --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: It doesn't simply get a reference to the ordinance as being a factor in the storeowner's decisions in any of the cases --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, what I just read --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Except that it's against the law.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But that's just in one case.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And there's nothing I gather in the other cases.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, in the other cases, I think the Court made it clear to the lawyer, he wasn't (Inaudible) -- have that testimony.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: He was -- yes.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: And then in another place, the lawyer for the petitioners wanted to bring out that these people would have been served if it hadn't been for the ordinance and the Court cuts that off.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: What page is that Mr. Motley?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes sir. It's -- one on page 67 and one on page 168. The Court said at this point in the middle of that page, “Is there any question before the Court now?” And Mr. Hall, the lawyer for petitioner said, “If Your Honor pleases, we have asked Mr. Sterling (ph) about new various policy.” The Court - “That is not competent”. And then he took an exception.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What did the Alabama -- what did the appellate court say about that --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: The appellate --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: -- its evidentiary ruling?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What did the appellate court say about this evidentiary ruling?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: He -- they didn't say anything about that specific ruling. They ignored all the evidence of the State policy -- the State ordinance. And said, the owner here was exercising his right to choose his own customers. As to the ordinance, they said it has not been pleaded and therefore they wouldn't pass on that but there is a statute in Alabama which requires the Court to take judicial notice of an ordinance.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But the Alabama Supreme Court ruled they didn't have to.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: The Court of Appeals of Alabama.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Court of Appeals, yes.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, they said that it should have been pleaded that we should have by some pleading set forth that ordinance. Well of course, I don't believe that's true because number one, you could take judicial notice of it, and number two, the petitioner said my whole defense is there. And certainly, that was before the Court when it reviewed the case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: How is the State in a position despite all that? How was the State in a position to say that this is voluntary action of an owner when he'd be a criminal under that state law if he did?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, of course they're not and I -- the Court in making its decision just ignored all of the evidence here that the police came in, that the storeowner did not call the police that even after the police arrived, the storeowners did not request the arrest. And the officer was asked in every case, “Was an arrest requested?” So it's clear I think that the State was acting in this case to enforce a state policy regardless of what the owner's policy might have been.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: In the absence of an or -- of the ordinance, would we have the Garner case here? Is this Garner?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes. I think that here, the -- as far as the free speech -- are you saying or the donor with respect to no evidence.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: No evidence.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, I think that in each of these cases, the petitioners were asked to leave the store by some representative of the store and in that sense, this evidence that they were asked to leave.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Now, as to the Garner case, what we say with reference to that is that this situation is like the opinion of Mr. Justice Harlan in Garner that here, the storeowners were apparently willing to endure this controversy. They did not call the police. When the police came, they didn't ask if they'd be arrested. And so these petitioners were there demonstrating against the state's policy of enforcing segregation and they demonstrated in this manner by sitting themselves at the counter, quietly asking for service like anyone else who as white does. And the owner apparently was willing to, as I say, endure this kind of controversy in this manner. If he was not, he obviously would have called the police. When the police arrived, he would have said I wanted them arrested. And there's no question that they were peaceful. There were only two in each store.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Mrs. Motley, do I understand (Inaudible) to say that the (Inaudible) in the application?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: No sir. There -- Gober was tried and his companion was Davis. Davis was not separately tried. The testimony as to Gober was stipulated as a testimony as to Davis.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Not to other?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: No sir. And then the next two came up. They were in a different store. One was tried, the testimony as to that -- defendant was stipulated as to his companion and so forth.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Now with reference to the (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. After that, he didn't mention the ordinance again. But as I say, there's a state statute which requires the courts to take judicial notice of ordinances in cities of a certain size of which Birmingham certainly is one.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Yes but I understand that but there is no (Inaudible) by counsel.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Or the defendant in the other case.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And were these cases tried in sequence (Voice Overlap)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, one after the other. They were --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Before the same judge?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Before the same judge --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Same counsel?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes sir, same counsel.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Same day?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: They were treated as a whole. The motions were all identical --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Filed all in the same day then?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, they were. I believe they were. And the sentencing was a joint sentencing for all of them. They were treated as one case. I don't think there's any question about that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: On page 26 Mrs. Motley --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Am I wrong in agreeing to the judge's ruling as overruling the objection to the inquiries to the segregation ordinances -- overruling (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: I think that's some confusion in the record --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: When --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible) three times now and it's the reason to my question.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, it's very --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I advise that -- I want to object as to Mr. Davis' counsel to the State, I want to object to the records, to the segregation ordinance, this ordinance has nothing to do with the manner of segregation. I think he'd need a trespass statute.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And the Court -- wouldn't it apply equally to everybody and I take it that refers to the segregation ordain -- ordinance. I overrule the objection.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, but then Mr. Harlan, on -- to ask about the ordinance and he then said that wasn't competent. And I think that what he intended to say was that he was sustaining the objection of the city's -- city attorney to the introduction of any testimony concerning the ordinance. It's very confusing. I was not at the trial and the way I read it here this is what he was really doing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: He didn't -- no objection, aren't they?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: He did not -- would've intended that?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. He did not let him put in that evidence regarding the ordinance. So what he was doing was sustaining the city's objection to the introduction of testimony regarding the effect of the ordinance.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible) confusing to me. Mr. Hall thought he had an opening --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- and he promptly asked the question, “Are you aware of the ordinance?” And the witness promptly answered, “I've heard of it”.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And then Mr. Davis promptly objected again the judge sustaining objection. What's confusing about that?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, I think that what the Court was saying that he didn't want any testimony regarding that ordinance in this record. That's the way I read it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, I wonder, can't it be read on the other way?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, it could be certain if you read it to me, my objections is as to what the judge says as to what the ordinance (Inaudible) use the question of the law to distinguish what effect the ordinance had on the actions of the (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, I think it's subject to that interpretation, yes.f

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: I thought the judge had said on the page before though, page 25, and the policy wouldn't be immaterial anyways if (Inaudible) -- was that the manager?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes. I think at that point he was talking about Kress' policy --

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: That was (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- the man at the storeowner's policy.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: But on the law, not on the policy. He was judge -- yes, but that was referring to the ordinance rather than the policy of the manager if he said that manager's policy would not be material.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, they were talking about both I think at this point. He said that the manager's policy was not material. He also said that the ordinance was not. As I understand the trial judge, he didn't want to hear that this is -- would serve these people notwithstanding the ordinance. He didn't want to hear that either.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: He was the manager, was he?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, he was the president or vice president of the business, the person who --

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: He's the one that told them to leave the store.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. I think I would like to save the remaining portion of my time for the rebuttal.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: May I ask one question?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Does it show one of those that has now abandoned the old type of --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Not that I know of sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It's not in that group.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: No sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Davis.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: May it please the Court. First, if I might clarify one or two items that have just gotten into this matter, we've been discussing here the unusual circumstances of the Court's ruling and I think simply it was a slip of the tongue. You'll notice Mr. Hall and the judge have been discussing this matter from page 24 through 26. And I think the judge forgot the frame of the court -- the form of the question of the objection at this point. It was simply going along with the thing and overruling. When he says overrule, I think he means the “overrule” is a general approach to this thing that you're getting into the surrounding or -- irrelevant matters. But another thing that I want to mention before I go into what I had in mind, addressing the Court on is the propositions that counsel has said apparently they were willing to tolerate this condition. And I believe in briefs, they mentioned the proposition of economic give and take. And I might throw this question out for an answer if they desire to answer it. Why would the storeowners have turned out the lights if they want them to sit there and tolerate this? Why did they ask them to leave if they were willing to tolerate this? Why did they say we cannot serve you here if they're willing to tolerate it?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, he just said immediately before, he cannot serve them there. He told them it would be against the law to serve them.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I would like to go back to that page 24, Mr. Justice Black, and follow that if we may of the language, the comptroller, both Pizitz -- I believe his name is Mr. Gotlinger who was testifying at the time and this was cross-examination. And Mr. Hall is asking Mr. Gotlinger, what some other person said? What Mr. Pizitz, Dick Pizitz, the assistant -- I believe the assistant of the president. He said, “What did he tell them?” He told them, “They couldn't serve there and we had facilities in the basement to serve them”. He told them that it would be against the law to serve them there. He told them it would be against the law to serve them there. What law did he have in mind, do you know? Answer - I don't know. Question - He didn't say it was against the (Inaudible) policy to serve them there and this is the key. He used the term we cannot serve you here. Question - And you assumed that he meant it that it was against the law? I assumed that. So all of these there is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Mr. Davis.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Do you suppose that any merchant in Birmingham even if he hadn't been there as long as he possessed the store there. He didn't know it was against the city law to serve white and colored together.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Mr. Justice Black, it's my sincere thought on this matter that the ordinance never comes in to the thought process.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understand that but is there --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I can make a confession --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: How can anybody think in Birmingham just live there and knows it but Mr. Pizitz's store all these years didn't know that it was against the law to serve them together. That may be legal. You may have a right to do it legally but how could anybody be assumed not to know it when it was on the books and that -- when its been the practice since time immemorial.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, of course I'll say this, I believe there are some 40 odd years and I have never heard of it being enforced.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, you've only heard of it being enforced because it was obeyed.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: That's a possibility.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But certainly it's not obeyed, its just hadn't been (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: But I think it was obeyed long before it became an ordinance.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The merchant wouldn't dare to break it even if he wanted to, wouldn't he?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I think it goes back to the question of which comes first, the chicken or the egg.Wasn't that the custom existing at the time the ordinance went into effect so there was no occasion for any change. But I would say this to his honor and I'm a little bit ashamed to admit it, I was only with the city attorney's office a few months when this case came up. I didn't know there was such an ordinance on that book and I didn't think there was and when Mr. Hall got into this question, he'd been shooting constitutional objections in every direction. I thought this was another one of these generalized objections that segregation was an issue. He had no city code on his hand. He was citing it --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You hadn't been a merchant running a restaurant, merchants running a restaurant know who can they serve and who they can't.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, let's get back if I may to the basic question that I think is basic in this question -- in this whole controversy. First, what were the issues in this case? We filed a complaint charging one thing that they trespassed after warning. They concede that in each case the evidence shows --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes, but it (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- that they didn't.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But they hadn't trespassed after warning. They would've been -- if the man had not warned them, it could've been against the law from -- to go in there and sit there.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, we have a void ordinance which we concede is void. I've never known of the thing -- a case yet --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, the merchant --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- before (Inaudible) come in.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: A merchant can't be put at the risk of having -- saying that maybe they'll hold it void for him to set up against the validation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I personally do not see how in this case, it's possible to say for anybody to reach a conclusion in a matter of this importance that this merchant acted because he wanted to rather than because it was against the law. As far as I'm concerned, I see no answer to that thing.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I think there is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Now, I see a great difference. I don't misunderstand it. I see a great difference for myself when a merchant deciding he doesn't want to sell to somebody. I don't care what the ground is. Deciding he won't sell them and not sell them. And having a law that tells him he can't sell them and then say, “Well, because of his own choice he didn't sell them”.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, Mr. Justice Black, my answer to that is this. You of course are familiar with the locality of Birmingham and --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: You know that there's an abundance of attorneys down in the City of Birmingham and notwithstanding this whole expression you can't fight City Hall. You know that many of them down there do fight City Hall and if they wanted to for a moment --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Many of them fight City Hall on this issue?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: On this issue?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: On this issue.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir, but many others and if the merchant wanted to bring this thing ahead and see what their rights were, well I think it would've been very long ago. But again, I think it goes back to the question that custom preceded the ordinance and the ordinance didn't bring on any occasion whereby they should change that custom but we concede that the ordinance was invalid --

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: Well, in any case --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- theres no issue on that.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: In any case, you have the State putting a person in jail. What was the sentence here? Was it prison?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Oh, 30 days --

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: Any -- can either case whether it's the proprietor doesn't like Negroes in his store or whether the city ordinance says that he shan't serve. In either case you have a state putting a man in prison because he is a Negro shopping in a certain place, isn't that right?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, no sir. I don't think so. He's put in jail because he trespassed after he was told he was not to remain there --

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: No, that's a matter as to (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- which he actually admit, which (Voice Overlap).

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible) managed as to --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: But -- what the point is, and I don't think we have yet touched the basic point in that in the City of Birmingham's case.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: Do you think a retail store is like a man's home.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Very definitely. I think there are some factual differences certainly. But as far as the law and the powers out of the constitution are concerned, we think private property is private property whether it's in your home, whether it's in your office or whether it's in a small store or big store. But the prime question that I think is of importance for this case is, what were the issues?

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: I suppose if you're right on that then what you do would be to use the constitution to drive the wedges of segregation deep into all of our society.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir. I construe the constitution as a limitation of power and not some affirmative instrument to use to do positive things but a limitation upon the states not to give anybody --

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- positive power.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: But if you can use private property in this way and what you will do would be to use it to -- in a constitutional way to engraft segregation as the way it wanted.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, some people use the word segregation, some use the expression of racial discrimination. I prefer the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: Constitutional Clause versus Equal Protection.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes, I further think that racial discrimination of a different sense, racial choice or racial selectivity. I know that an Irish man prefer Irish man or at least I've heard that, I'm not Irish. I know there's a Chinese community in our hometown.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: But you get a different problem when you go to a judge and say, “Put this man in jail because he violated my prejudice”.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir. I don't think its prejudice. It's a personal taste. I'm told by responsible neighbors, they have segregation among themselves in our city. You say -- some people have feeling, I think that the Negroes don't prosper in the South. Well, I can assure this Court in my hometown they have beautiful residential districts and I feel right ashamed when I see some of their Cadillac's, new ones and I have a 10-year-old automobile. That's the rule and not the exception to the rule that I make to this Court. But the issue we're getting out here is how was this case tried out in the state court? To me, it was just another lawsuit. We filed a complaint saying, “Well, you didn't leave when you were asked to”. Trespass after warning, a simple city ordinance. They came along and they filed a motion to strike the complaint. Supreme Court decided authority and two cases have been in this Court, one is (Inaudible) versus Birmingham and the other, Taylor versus Birmingham. A motion to strike in this type of action which is a quasi civil action does not serve the purpose to attack the validity or the sufficiency of the complaint. They don't say that it does. They -- haven't taken an issue with that proposition. The motion to strike went out the window whether they come along with the next, to set demurs in this case. The Court of Appeals in Alabama said two things, first, they were all general and we have a code section, this issue must file a specific demurs specified forth in the complaint you're objecting to and not be general. The Court of Appeals said every one of those were general. In addition to that, they raised certain constitutional objections, they said, “As applied to this defendant, this press -- this ordinance does so and so and so”. Well, the Court said, “That was a speaking demurrer because it required evidence”. It didn't appear on his face how it was going to affect a particular defendant. They took evidence. That was a speaking demurrer. They haven't denied that that was -- or claimed that that was an erroneous ruling. So what do we normally come to than a plea of general issue? There they trespassed after warning. Isn't that the only question we had in the trial court? We're charged they did, they say, “We didn't.” Now they come along and say all but the city was negative state action. Under what theory are we required to make it if anything? They drew the boundary lines in this dispute. They didn't file a form or any special plea. They brought up nothing of that nature that the Court could adjudicate. This Court in Hill versus Mendenhall, about -- in 88 U.S., announced the fundamental question of pleadings, pleadings apprise the party on the Court of the facts and issue, the Court that it may declare the law in the parties that they may know what to meet by their proof. And here, we're sitting here with the general issue to how they say, “Well, you can take judicial notice”. They're asking the lower court to act as counsel. When they say that -- judicial notice is not a substitute for pleading if I may (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Do you require pleadings in a criminal case?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir, but these are quasi civil.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But do you say -- yes, but do require pleadings?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: What is -- what are the pleadings that you require?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Pardon? I think its quasi criminal but --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I beg your pardon. I didn't --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Mr. Breckenridge was saying, this is case is quasi criminal and you asked the question what pleadings are required in criminal cases.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, its criminal when you go to jail, isn't it? A violation of the law --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Not under the Alabama decisions interpreting these matters Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You mean not under the labor legislative?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Pardon sir?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You mean not under the labor legislative but these are cases, are they not, if a man can be sent to jail for prison for six months?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And fined how much?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir. A hundred dollars.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: $100 and 180 days.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Tried first in the city policeman.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I don't contend that they're not penal. They're punished.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: They're not criminal law you say?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Under the Supreme Court decision in the State of Alabama, they are quasi criminal. The rules of civil proceedings apply on appeal. Your civil rules apply. They must assign error and argue if the Court of Appeals is going to hear it on the Supreme Court.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: What effect does a plea of not guilty have? What issues does it brace?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: The general issue is --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: In a case of --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- they didn't do it.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: In a case of this kind.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: They didn't do it.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, they can enter, yes, but what issues can be raised under the plea of not guilty.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Oh, the question that they didn't do the act complained of then the complaint --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What is that -- what is required as I understand it, it used to be was that the State or the city proved its case beyond a reasonable doubt every element in it. One element if you were trying to trespass after warning would be rule beyond a reasonable doubt that the owner of his own choice not because he had a gun in his bag had directed the man to leave, so that would be an issue in the case, wouldn't it?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: If it were brought up by a plea or a defense what --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: We are not guilty.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Not guilty claims (Inaudible).

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: If I may say this, Mr. Justice Black, I think you're going in -- to watch the defense to this trespass saying all we're involved is, it is proving that he did put the ordinance --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- that says you must not do.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But the ordinance is based on the principle I suppose that he was ordered by the owner of his own choice with his own knowledge making up his own mind not to stay there, isn't it?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I would assume that someone wants to tell him --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Suppose they didn't do that? Supposed you have to charge --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- if you believe from the evidence in this case on the plea of not guilty. Do you believe from the evidence in this case that the owner did not himself do this because he wanted to do it but because there was a man standing behind him with a gun in his bag? They asked to charge it then. There was evidence to that effect, could you convict?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I think once you prove the simple fact, he was told to leave the premises and didn't --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You could --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- the State has carried its burden and then the defense takes over.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You could convict even though the evidence showed beyond the shadow of a doubt that the man had a gun in his back when he did it that somebody telling him, “You've got to do this”?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: You have to tell him to get off the premises?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir that would not be a voluntary act.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, it's the same thing is it not, always been recognized if the law tells you that you can't do something.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, you're assuming of course that they acted under this invalid law and I am assuming that --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I'm not assuming.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: (Inaudible) we hadn't filed a charge.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I'm assuming that the State has to show that they acted and they exercised their own judgment and their own choice in ordering him to get out. And what you have here is that there's a law standing there, they have them in debate that if they don't order them to get out there, they'll go to jail themselves.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, as I interpret Your Honor's question or statement, it looks like that the State in prosecuting any case, somebody shoot somebody else, they don't have to show that it -- they voluntarily did it, they're going to have to show that they were not insane that the State has got the negative ever defensive element.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But they get --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: But this is purely defensive I think what is on (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's true beyond a reasonable doubt in every allegation but that doesn't include that allegation I think to that ordinance.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Oh, the allegation simply says that he failed to refuse after being warned (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, it would certainly be a bad ordinance wouldn't it if it was based on the fact that the owner did that because the law told him to. That would be a bad order, wouldn't it, void?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I think the ordinance is telling he's got to make that election as bad and void, yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That would be void?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir. And I think (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I think that -- that's an essential element as that he did it himself, not because the State made it, I would think.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, if that were the law that in every case and I'm not a prosecutor that you probably guess, I'm a land attorney --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- in the City of Birmingham. But I don't think the law places any burden on the prosecution to come along in negative every possible defense it may creep into a situation. As I see this, this is a defensive matter and you'll notice in the discussion plea, Mr. Hall and the Court -- Mr. Hall says first, “That if the had not gone in to Pizitz's store and Mrs. Pizitz have not told them to get out the store”, and that's on page 24 and 25. He says, “We wouldn't be here”. And the Court says, “Well, is there any (Inaudible) between you and the city on this?” In other words if Pizitz hadn't told them to get out, well, they wouldn't have been prosecuted and he says, “I don't know”. Then the Court says this, “In the issue in this thing whether they trespassed after warning”. And he said, “I think so Mr. Hall”, the petition for counsel -- counsel for petitioners. Then the Court says one other thing, “Well, isn't that issue, isn't that the thing that you mean?” And what did he say, “It certainly is Your Honor”. So how could we be more doubly sure of what we're trying in this case? What the issues were? How we were to fix the boundary lines of the dispute and not only by the pleadings but by the admission of the counsel himself that the boundary lines are -- is -- has there been a trespass committed? Isn't that the issue? Isn't that the thing which is the main --?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: After warning.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Pardon sir?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: After warning.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, is there a -- anything said (Inaudible)? Are you saying that the rules of procedure applicable to this kind of a proceeding required the defendant to affirmatively -- to raise this sort of a defense based on the Constitution?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Oh, very definitely, very definitely.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And that if he raised it in a proper form, are you saying that he had the burden of carrying that defense?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, it's a matter that he had searched. He has that burden of proceeding with it at least.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And what was the burden on the -- what degree of proof is the case subject to in this kind of a proceeding, beyond a reasonable doubt or is it just a preponderance of the evidence or what is it?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: It is beyond a reasonable doubt but may I say this at this point? They have cited the Thompson versus Louisville case and of course the Garner case and we would like to join them in citing those two cases. The rule that this Court enunciated and as I recall correctly in Garner was that the test is not the sufficiency of the evidence before the trial court but whether there was any evidence before the trial court. They concede the evidence is positive in each case. The employee of a store ordered them the premises. So we have no issue there really.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Did the Court of Appeals get in considering the exclusion of the evidence that was excluded --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No, it was not argued. It's mentioned in the Court's opinion that they had five assignments of error which they must -- if they had to rely on them, assigned as error and argued. The fifth, what it was, some question about evidence. I believe it was in the evidence sufficed the prejudice, that's my recollection but it appears in each case, the assignment of errors. But they didn't even argue that. They didn't argue any question of evidence, whatever in the case. They simply argued that demonstrations were -- that there were demonstrations, there were symbols of communications or symbols of something rather. And if you know, the Court took off on one or two of those arguments. But the only thing they argued before the Court of Appeals was the overruling of the -- and they didn't argue this, they've cited this error. You're reeling a motion to strike complaint that emerged and they file this motion to exclude the evidence, a motion for new trial. And that -- unless this motion excludes the evidence, may I say this, this question of state action gets into it. If you'll notice, there are 8 grounds, grounds 2, 3, 4, 7, and 8 say this if I may point out to the Court and invite your attention to them. I think on page 6 you'll find the first one in the Gober case (Inauidble). In five of those grounds, they say it's not the policy of the City of Birmingham or the State of Alabama. They say it's the policy of the customer and the usage of (Inaudible) or whatever particular department store was involved that has deprived them of their Fourteenth Amendment yearns. The other three grounds, they don't attribute to fall through -- direct in accusing things as to anyone but that simply was a confirmation as I view it and what the simple issue was in the trial court in the first place, did they or didn't they trespass after somebody told them to get off the premises. And we had no other question and they didn't bring that up until they got to this Court. But the local counsel, there were five of them who were experts in this matter. You will notice that motion to exclude the evidence five times of the eight grounds they assert. It says it was the policy of the store or the policy custom in usage of the store, or the custom or the preponderant number of department stores in the City of Birmingham that deprived them of their rights. So we were not arguing any constitutional issues in this thing. But since they brought them up, I think right here, the local counsels directs them to the policy of the stores themselves as confessed with the State and the city. It fairly -- clearly answers the question.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Is your position that the federal questions were not adequately raised below?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Not raised at all sir. Well, adequately perhaps is a better way to put it but in the demurrers they were not properly raised in a motion to strike, that doesn't even lie and there had been two cases from Alabama for this Court, the (Inaudible) versus Birmingham and Taylor versus Birmingham where the petition for certiorari was denied that's involved. And then after the evidence was taken now we went to issue on that several question. After the evidence was there and they come along and filed a motion to exclude the evidence never suggesting that the evidence was sufficient in any way. They simply say the evidence shows that these stores were exercising these policies.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I recall that in --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Is that the motion (Inaudible)?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Which ground? Please sir, you --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Well, look at page 12 of the record.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: And the judgment of the Court granted below (Inaudible) each defendants had the evidence in this case, the evidence to go on the specific there is -- this ruling, to overrule the objection and then on (Inaudible). Is that properly raised the issue to (Inaudible) the case?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I would not think so. The case had already been tried at this point Your Honor.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: But you didn't try it yourself. First, in the (Inaudible) pleadings been demurred and then (Inaudible) into evidence. And was it to exclude evidence that (Inaudible) reference to constitutional issue?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Constant reference, yes sir. Yes sir, they were constantly referring to constitutional proposition but not once did they come in with a formal plea or a special plea as this Court has said in the Mendenhall -- Hill versus Mendenhall. How would any lawyer know how to prepare a case if they were not to have pleadings?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You consider -- you may finish that answer.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Davis, you may continue your arguments.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Thank you sir. May it please the Court. One other question which was injected into the controversy here and I say here because I respect to submit it was never injected under the controversy in the state courts. Was this question a police action? As I mentioned earlier in Alabama, you assigned to their appellate courts your errors that you wish the Court to consider. Now the Supreme Court rules specifically state that if you don't assign them or don't argue them they won't consider those grounds. But these acts considered -- are injected into the thing by appellate counsel and not by a local counsel in the state courts. But it has been injected under the thing here which I don't think it's a proper forum for it without the Court having first had a chance to rule on it. They bring up the question how the arrest were effectuated and brought about. Again, I say that the plea was a simple general denial. Had we known that this was one of the channels that they wanted to proceed on by the pleadings then we would have brought the police records to Court? We could have mastered up the complaint records. We could have looked for witnesses to say or to find out who made this report to the police. The police didn't come there through some form of mesmerism. Someone initiated this thing but it was not upon us to explain that situation under a simple general issue in this matter.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, we've had a good many of these sit-in cases where the police did initiate it and we had to reverse it for that reason.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I think that would be the problem, right.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: So, it is a mesmerism, that's a (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I think that would be a proper action. But I think this that we've got to try the case within the framework of the pleadings. We can't shoot mugshot in every direction and hope one will hit here and one will hit there. Going back to your Hill versus Mendenhall case, the fundamental principle of pleadings is to define the issue so the Court may declare the law and the parties may know what to meet by their proof and agreement. But in any event, the petitioners themselves, elicited from the officers who were testifying that for us who's in possessed, when the arresting officer arrived, he or his superior is talking with people in the store presumably witnesses who had seen this thing, getting information and they directed the arresting officer to make the arrest. They brought it out also that in Laughlin's, one of the officers through cross-examination, one of his superior officers have been told that two of these volunteers have been told to leave and refused. So it -- there was a duty on him to make an arrest. An ordinance had been violated. And in the case of (Inaudible) they -- I think as they have brought it out in the trial court, Mrs. Evans, the (Inaudible) manager told the officer she had told the two boys to leave and they refused and that in his presence she told them. So in the presence of the officer, the offense was committed. But I say again and I hope the Court would adhere to its rulings. It has adopted in the past that these matters which are not before a state court will not come up here for a first hearing. In other words, not let them come here to try the case.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Davis, may --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: May I ask if in your -- if in your brief, you have cited the statute which compels a special pleading in this case to raise these questions?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir, its Title VII.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: It's in your brief is it?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, if it's in your brief, that's all.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: And it relates to civil cases and I also spell out that these are quasi criminal cases that all your rules of evidence that which apply in civil cases apply in quasi criminal cases to appellate rules that apply into civil cases apply in quasi criminal cases. It is a civil proceeding. Now the Court of Alabama whether rightly or wrongly is held that an offense against the city ordinance does not rise for the dignity of a crime whether that is its philosophy in this matter, I don't know. They don't go into it.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: This isn't an ordinance. This is a state law, isn't it?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, this is a city ordinance they were prosecuted on this.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Oh yes, yes, I remember.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: City of Birmingham's ordinance.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: But in any event, this Court have said --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That may be the rule --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- to which you referred in your brief, I --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Pardon sir?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You said there was rule in your brief about the --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: It's the code section in volume -- in Title VII.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, that is cited in your brief here?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir, yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, if you don't know, that's alright, I'll find it.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: But it is Title VII and I think its 235, let's see -- Court of Alabama, 225. I copied a portion of it on page 5 --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Page 5.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Or at least I make a reference to it at that point. I think it's in Shuttlesworth case perhaps it was that I copied part of it. But --

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: (Inaudible) the best way to present the evidence that he offers being (Inaudible) at the department store?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: He went into the policy of other stores, I recall and I think the Government suggested that is being tantamount to an attempted to show the city ordinance. I made the objection that the policy of the other stores would not be involved. He was going not in Birmingham where this case came up but into other areas and I think the courts there it was and perhaps erroneously I won't comment on it, that policy had nothing to do with it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It was suggested, wasn't it?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I believe he said, aren't we trying -- a question here whether he violated this ordinance. But I think they brought nothing before the Court in the nature of a formal plea to suggest these things.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you what kind of special plea would have been filed to raise the issue of that -- this man did not do this because of his own choice but because the law forced him to do it? What kind of plea? I -- probably it's been changed since I was there, that I do not recall.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, it would just be a plea setting up affirmatively these defensive matters for that they exist in the City of Birmingham, an ordinance that define this Section so and so and that it was in compliance with this ordinance as the storeowner operate -- made this request to leave or direct them to leave. And so we'll put it in issue.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You mean that the burden of proof would have been put in him on the Alabama law to prove that the man actually gave the order of his own choice?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I don't conceive the choice is an issue. The ordinance says, if a man tells you to get off his property, you must leave. If you don't, you violate the ordinance. If that's the ordinance as Your Honor is thinking of.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, what you are saying is that a man -- whether the man did it because the law compelled him to do it or whether he did it because he wanted to, it would be immaterial to prove any crime.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: What's the defensive matter, may I submit?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Defensive matter, that would be -- the burden would be put on him.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: That's right sir. But he hasn't even put it in issue. Irrespective of whose burden it is, it's not even an issue in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Have you cited any cases to that effect with reference to (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I cite a case to this Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The defendant --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I cited --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- affirmatively proving on his part that which is an essential element of the crime if assuming it shows an essential element of the crime.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, Your Honor perhaps I made this mistake. I assume that it was not a question for the State. To me, it's something to a plea of contributory negligence. I assume this Your Honor on a theory (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- an enemy.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You don't plea it contributory negligence down there to cases where the people had --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No, what I'm -- speaking of the same general situation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I suppose they'd be sent to jail for six months.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir, but basically you have the affirmative matter and defensive matter. You're the defendant in this thing --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I thought, our court there stopped very tenaciously to the idea that the business of Government whether it's a city or state to prove guilt beyond a reasonable doubt.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Proved that he committed the act charged.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Beyond a reasonable doubt?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir, yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, you wouldn't just have to prove that he shot somebody. You'd have to prove he shot him with malice and deliberate -- deliberation and so forth. He wouldn't have to plead any of that would he, as a defendant?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I don't know but I am --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I don't know that I'm a 100%, the Court decided on that proposition though it's not exactly the question here.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What about an alibi, does he have to plead especially an alibi?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir, because he pleads not guilty which means I didn't do it and if I can show that I was somewhere else at the time then certainly the jury can assume using his common experience as a common sense but if he was somewhere else he's sure didn't do it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, what he means when pleading guilty -- not guilty here of course this might be a state problem but I'm just interest -- I have to confess my interest to it, what he is charging is, I'm not guilty of -- he may say, I'm not guilty of being there.I'm not guilty of staying there or you may say I'm not guilty of staying there after the defendant -- after the owner of the property because he wanted to, not -- it's not because the State made him, ordered me to leave.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, our position is that he simply says, “I'm not guilty of what you say I did”. Now we say he did on -- there were two elements to what we say he did. Number one, he was on the premises, he was told to leave. But number two, he refused to leave.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Supposed he'd been told to leave by a man who came in from the street.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, they don't say that that was a fact. They concede that --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Of course it was.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- before he did it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Suppose it was.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I think I mentioned in my brief that if that situation existed, I didn't feel like the ordinance had been violated. But again --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You think the ordinance had been violated?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I would not think so and I mentioned that in my brief.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Oh, you weren't -- oh, I thought you were.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir. I said that in my brief. I didn't think it can be violated but they make no such contention in this case.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Davis, you cite your Alabama Code 1940, Title VII, Section 225 and you quote as follows - “The defendant may plead more pleas than one without unnecessary repetitions. And if he does, not rely solely on a denial of the various cause of action. It must plead especially the matter of defense”. Now that sounds to me more like a civil proceeding than a criminal proceeding --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Does this specifically apply to criminal cases?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: It is -- we might suppose that that particular Code Section applies by its own language to civil cases.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: To civil cases?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir. In our brief, we have developed its application to this case.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, what -- is there any case that holds that this are -- these civil rules apply to criminal cases?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Rules of evidence. We don't have a whole field of law on --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Is there any one case that you cite?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Not -- no sir. No sir. But under even criminal law if you wanted to come in and say the defendant was insane, you would put it in a plea of insanity. But If you want to plea there was an involuntary act where he's charged with perhaps murder, you want to show this involuntary act. You'd have to show something. You admittedly did it, it's more like a confession and avoidance, really. You admit you did the act that say you did what? And then we avoid the act even in criminal law.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, have you have a --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: That this happens to fall in a civil case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The elements of first degree murder down there are (Inaudible) -- premeditated killing with malice aforethought.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: That's right sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Do you mean that --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: It's presumed from the homicide --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That he does -- well, I'm not talking about what's the inference they could find from evidence that it was done with malice aforethought but do you mean that the burden is on the defendant to show the didn't do it with malice aforethought?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Of course, the burden is on him, yes sir. Well, I'd say this is a presumption. When I throw a weapon and I fired it to the man, they don't have to go beyond that and say, “Oh, but in his heart, his heart was black, he intended to do it”, while the evidence speaks for itself but if I want to show that somebody dropped me with something or has turned me into a pinch of insanity through some process, I would come along and set that up as a defense but certainly the State isn't going to say that all of these various defenses didn't happen --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But all of these --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- because they would never their case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If it's a question -- of course, it maybe too far from this but if -- but I have as to the -- if it's a question of showing and it was done with deliberation and malice forgetting the words presumption, the Court always charges a jury, doesn't it?You must find from the evidence that he did it deliberately and with malice aforethought and find it beyond a reasonable doubt --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I think that's (Inaudible) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- without regard to pleading.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I think that's even in the indictment, yes sir. So I think they have to -- they generally will show some motive involved with it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: My -- I would like to come back if I could to my question. Assuming that you asked for those to file a special plea and assuming that the State's proof was sufficient to withstand the motion for instructed burden of not guilty, would it be permissible for the defendant in examining -- in putting on his testimony to offer the proof that he said he wanted to offer with reference to Mr. Pizitz's policy.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Assuming that it was a proper form of plea or injected in to -- that in a proper issue, I think so.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I mean, assuming that he did not raise it by plea at all --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir, assuming (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Just like in cases here.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And that he was supposed to raise it. The law required him to raise it, you'll say. And that your proof or the State's proof was sufficient to go to the jury, could he in rebutting your proof of trespass on this, I guess you would have to prove that it was a trespass, wouldn't you?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir. That he remained after he was warned to leave.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Now assuming that in common colloquy, we told that you met a prima facie proof in a civil action.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I suppose it's not any such thing as criminal action. And you went -- you were able to go to the jury on that. Could he request that prima facie proof by offering proof that you tendered as I understand it on page 25 that Mr. Pizitz will assume had no such policy of segregation and that he wanted these people here, indeed he has indicted them to come in.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I think if he of course have put it in a form of a plea or any pleading as to it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I mean suppose he didn't plead it.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir. Then I quoted to it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The evidence wouldn't be admissible.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I'm certain not in Alabama.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The judge didn't go off on that ground.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I beg your pardon.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The judge didn't go off on that ground. He said it was immaterial.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, what he said was as I understood it, it was that the witness who was an employee of a store, his knowledge of the law was not competent really and later on he (Voice Overlap) material.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Mr. Pizitz's policies in the Court on 25, Mr. Pizitz's policy would not be material as I conceive this. That's what he said.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If that were true, it looks like to me that he would have said that you didn't plead this or the evidence is not admissible.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I think the Court was right in this particular instance where it was not made an issue and they had the right to drew up the boundary lines on this dispute. They have (Inaudible) -- they had been in more of these cases than I have.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That wouldn't be meeting your burden of showing a trespass. I wouldn't be trying to meet your burden -- I mean trying to counteract your burden, your proof.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir. We only had two elements to prove. One, they were told to leave and two, they refused and the only burden on the State -- on the city. Because they didn't file any plea -- they filed all sorts of other things, very extensive pleadings but elected to go to issue on the general denial.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Where can we find any discussion in any cases of the Supreme Court of Alabama or in any textbooks on the requirement to file special pleas in criminal cases in your State?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: In criminal cases.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: In criminal cases, yes.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Your Honor, we have in the code, forms of pleas to file any criminal cases. I think they assume that --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, why don't you cite them in your brief instead of citing this as a general language such as there is in 25 --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I stopped to the civil rules which we contend that this case comes under it and the Court of Appeals cited cases. It's in the opinion of the lower court, (Inaudible) versus State, that --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And that's the only time that such thing has been discussed in the history of Alabama?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir. I've cited two cases this morning where on appeal, your civil rules apply (Inaudible) versus State came to this Court on petition for certiorari. Senator Glen Taylors case versus City of Birmingham came to this Court. The petition for certiorari was denied. But it said in all of those cases, these things follow civil rules in Alabama, civil rules. They had another case before this one that came to this Court where the civil rules in the record that didn't get to certain questions (Inaudible) --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And that is because you call it a quasi criminal --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I never have really liked the word quasi criminal, how much is quasi? I don't --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, that's what I was wondering because Justice Black was asking you about a first degree murder case and you said that there, there would have to be a special pleading. So is that under the civil rules too?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir, I'm sure not. I think you cannot --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: What set of rules (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- under said common law rule under the same rule enunciated by an earlier court hearing in Hill versus Mendenhall that -- the part of is that it would be applied in what the issues are. The courts got to know it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Have you read the case of Leland versus Oregon. Do you happen to read it in studying this case where we held up the question as to whether they could put the burden of proof on the man to prove insanity in the Court -- majority of the Court held that they could in an insanity because it had always been done but pointed to the fact that this Court have held that this Government -- Federal Government must prove every essential element of a crime.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I think we must prove every essential element of the fact.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You think that -- there's no -- is there a difference, maybe I haven't quite understood. Is there a difference in your mind the pleadings required on an appeal from the Recorder's Court which this was, wasn't it?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir, yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is there a difference in your mind as to the pleadings a defendant is required to put in, in a case in the Recorder's Court where he's been convicted of a defense like this --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- to the rules that apply if he was charged with a state crime or misdemeanor?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I think generally, the same rule is applied but the courts have specifically stated that these cases are a quasi criminal case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I understand that because I (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I agree with you despite --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I don't know what that means.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir. I don't see but two basic differences in making a quasi criminal and not civil. One is that in civil, of course, the motion exclude the evidence won't apply it, must be a motion for the affirmative charge. There's another time at the time, it slipped through my mind but there's no real basic difference that I see in our pleadings down there.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I suppose that he has also proved that it was not a trespass to that.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, they should approve either one or two things. They weren't asked to leave or when they were asked, they did leave but they concede that those conditions existed and we met that burden of proof.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, wouldn't that be true with reference truth and that Mr. Pizitz did not want them to leave. He was acting under state's requirement of law.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Of course Mr. Pizitz himself didn't ask that somebody who was simply quoting him and saying what he assumed Mr. Pizitz meant in this thing.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Oh, what I mean, they offered to prove as I understand it (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: But they had put in a plea of state action, certainly, they would have been able to come in and establish that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I mean just in meeting your burden. Your burden must be proved that there was a trespass. It was up to them to prove there wasn't a trespass. That's what you say.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: In essence, yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Now, in trying to prove that there was not a trespass, it would have been appropriate for them to offer proof that Mr. Pizitz instead of ordering them out indeed wanted them to stay. But -- and now read -- told them to go out because it was the State law.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I would've said that, it would've been a very good false value -- it had Mr. Pizitz up there and testified to that and prior to that it could have pleaded to that effect and let the Court know that that was an issue in this case as well as the question did they or were they in fact ever told to leave and did in fact leave after they were told to. Those were the two issues that were there. They could have put the other issues in and in fact, I think it would have been advised to bring Mr. Pizitz to say, “Well, I want them to sit at the lunch counter.
