I'd like to have them here.” But this confound the city ordinance which I know is invalid (Inaudible) but that isn't in this record.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well of course, practically speaking, I suppose it would be from a -- was on a persuasive argument against construing the ordinance in such a way that the guilt or innocent could be determined in an unknown hidden motive which prompted the man to give the order, wouldn't it? That is -- that couldn't be a very satisfactory way, was it? I'm not saying that you haven't advocated that.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I'm just thinking about that as a rule of law.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I -- I'm not completely clear on what His Honor had referenced to --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What I mean is this. Suppose it does depend on -- so as you have to prove whether he did it for one reason or another. The only way you could do that would be to prove someway of what was in his mind (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Proof of mental operation, a mental operation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That would be a pretty difficult that --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I think it would be.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Sometimes the law resorts to that but not unless it has to do, does it?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir. The mental operations, I think we'd get into a rather dangerous (Inaudible).

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, Mr. Davis, suppose you had a simple assault with a deadly weapon case and the State proved that the defendant used a wrench let's say and assaulted a person with it and then arrested. That would incorporate all the things that are ordinarily necessary in order to prove an assault with deadly weapon, wouldn't it?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Now, do -- if the -- suppose if the defendant wants to prove self-defense, is he obliged under your law to file a special pleading?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Not in writing, it's my impression in the criminal side.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, do these rules make a distinction between writing and orally?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, the pleadings must be filed in writing exactly speaking --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, why didn't you differentiate between them?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I don't know on the criminal level that's why I went to some extent to point out these things were civil in my brief because I think that rule -- we've traveled pretty much on the common law in the criminal cases. We simply prescribed the forms of pleas in criminal cases if you want to put them in issue. Self-defense I think is just a matter of argument and of course it's -- it would -- I don't think it would even require a plea because the events means that he did it without some provocation. In other words, its negative in its sense -- in itself, the self-defense.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But don't you get right back the same thing here (Voice Overlap)?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I don't think so.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Will you prove that he was told to leave and he doesn't leave? He says there was a reason other than a lawful reason as to why this man put me off his property?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, I --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Isn't that equivalent? You said a little while back that it was equivalent criminal contributory negligence. Well now, why isn't that just as much a plea under the general issue as self-defense?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Your Honor, I can't answer your question in all of these fringed areas. I will just answer -- I admit I can't.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: That's fine.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Mr. Davis ask you this? You refer to your ordinance which is in the record at page 58, would it not be a violation of your ordinance as agreed on its face or any of these defendants to have refused to leave after being warned by the police independently of what the storeowner said?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I would not go that far personally sir.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: But doesn't the State -- doesn't the ordinance say that?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: It said that after being warned, I think we have to assume it comes in our Code out of the chapter heading of trespass and of course trespass assumes that there is a proprietary address and I don't know how we could assume that the police officer had any proprietary interest.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Except the --

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Heading of trespass.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Except the ordinance itself does not require the warning to be by the owner.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I think by implication, you'd have to construe it that way and its context as a trespass ordinance. Someone in possession or some agent, designee or a servant of the person in possession or at least Mr. Pizitz's greater -- must be greater than the customer who is directed to leave.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: In other words, what you're saying here is that the ordinance doesn't say so but it would have to be construed that way.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: I think he would have.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: But wasn't the complaint here based upon the ordinance and the complaint itself also didn't say anything about the owner. So isn't the non-guilty plea then putting an issue -- all that was put in an issue by the complaint?You refer to the complaint? The complaint also doesn't say who did the warning but just says after being warned not to do so.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Well, that follow the language of the ordinance after --

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: -- being warned.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: So from the standpoint of the pleadings going back to the point which we have been discussing, didn't the not guilty plea put an issue to everything that the complaint alleged.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: That's right sir, yes sir. And if someone other than a person in authority had ordered them off, that would be an issue. We must prove that they were warned and I think it goes without saying that a proprietary address must be involved in that person warning.

turns.name: ARTHUR J. GOLDBERG
turns.text: Well, must you prove then that they were warned and in fact ordered off by the defendant and not by the State -- by the owner and not by the State?

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: No sir. I think the evidence showed that these were employees which they concede. They have never denied that. They don't raise the issue to some stranger or some policeman ordered them off the premises. And I don't think the officers could order them off. They took the reports after talking with the people there.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Very well Mr. Davis.

turns.name: WATTS E. DAVIS
turns.text: Thank you sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mrs. Motley.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, I think the controlling consideration is whether the court below or the highest court of the State which could have rendered an opinion or did render an opinion, considered and disposed of the constitutional issues which we are here presenting to this Court. And on page 61 of the record, the Court in the last paragraph of its opinion says, “Counsel has argued among other matters, various faces of constitutional law particularly as affected by the Fourteenth Amendment of the Federal Constitution such as freedom of speech”, in regard to which counsel state, “What has become known as a sit-in is a different but well-understood symbol, meaningful method of communication”. And the Court says, “Counsel has also referred to cases pertaining to restrictive covenants. We consider such principles entirely inapplicable to the present case.” And on page 60 of the record, they point out that they can consider in this type of proceeding because the civil rules govern on appeal, the matters raised and argued in the brief. And so --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Where does that appear on the -- on the page, was it --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: In brief for counsel -- in brief, counsel for appellant argues that the complaint is insufficient -- is insufficient and not setting forth -- I'm sorry, that's not it. Here, it's on page 59.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: This being an appeal from a conviction for violating a city ordinance. It is quasi criminal in nature, and subject to rules governing civil appeals. Accordingly, we will limit our review to errors assigned and argued in appellant's brief. And of course, this is cited in number of Alabama cases.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Does that mean that in -- I think maybe it does that in criminal cases now, what they call criminal state cases, they're not required to file assignments of their own. I'm sure that's true with capital cases. Is that the distinction that this opinion is drawing?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, I really --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: I really don't know.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- this is appeal from the Recorder's Court, they're not required to assign error?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, it appears if that's what they mean that all you have to do is to raise certain -- well, accordingly, we will limit our review to errors assigned so that errors were assigned --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And argued in the brief.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- and argued in appellant's brief.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: As a matter of fact, (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. And these matters and particularly the Fourteenth Amendment questions was certainly assigned as error and argued in our brief and considered by the Court and disposed of by the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible) that even if by (Inaudible) future points it makes. And in fact the Supreme Court paid no attention in considering everything that should be raised in the pleadings (Inaudible) where we don't have the before us.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Procedural right of error.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But that was -- the Court said not to consider the -- there was no issues as to that (Inaudible).

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, and on that point, we say you have the statute which says that the Court must take judicial notice --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But the court didn't.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- but it didn't. But this Court can take judicial notice of --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes. We've cite it in our brief.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, I believe it is.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Then it wasn't (Inaudible) in the Court that they entered.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Our petition for cert -- I'm sorry, I believe --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- in our petition for cert.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What would be then (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes. Well, what I'm saying is that here, the Court was required by the State law to take judicial notice of this ordinance and --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: (Inaudible) like to say that, (Inaudible) Court said that no issue about the ordinance that was made. An issue has been raised and that the appeal to introduce to the Court (Inaudible) will be required judicial notice to say that issue (Inaudible)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, I think really, we did more than that. We said it to the Court, our whole defense is the ordinance and I think that when we said that, we were saying everything that we were required to with this kind of proceeding to advise the Court and the other side of what our theory was.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: And --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- into pleadings in the state court of Alabama that you challenge the ordinance?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: In the brief, I believe they did mention it because the Court itself says that it was not raised by a pleading. You're here arguing it but it wasn't raised by a pleading. That's the way they treated it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But did they in fact consider it?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, they considered it in the sense that they said, “Here, you are arguing it and you didn't put it in a pleading. We think it should have been raised by a plea.”

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right but then they -- as to that ordinance. But then they went on to decide that this case was not governed by the restrictive covenant cases. And the only reason we're assigning the ordinance was to show that there was state action and violation of the Fourteenth Amendment. But again, even if the ordinance were not involved here, we would still be properly before this Court on Shelley against Kraemer because there was this massive state policy of racial segregation which we say compel the owner to put these people out and the State cannot escape responsibility for that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I would like to ask you two questions. From your brief in the Supreme Court, did you raise specifically the exclusion or the alleged exclusion of inquiry respecting the State ordinance, the city ordinance?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: I don't recall.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: As error, did you raise that as error?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Just a moment. I don't believe it was Your Honor raised as such.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Were they -- the assignment of error into the --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: The exclusion of the test of the evidence after --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's -- I'm talking about the brief.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: In the brief?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: In the brief. It was not raised (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Now, that I don't have a copy of -- with here so I can't answer that with definiteness that it was so raised.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I wonder with the permission of the Chief Justice you would leave a copy of the brief in the Supreme Court.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes, we will. We'll try to get a copy of the brief that was presented in the Supreme Court of Alabama. Now, I wanted to point out with reference to a question asked me earlier by Mr. Justice Goldberg. There was in the record a stipulation that the rulings made in the first case would apply to all other cases and that appears on page 106 of the record and on page 122 of the record. Not just the pleadings were to be the same but the rulings which he has made in the first case were the rulings in the other cases and that's why they didn't again raise that question as to the ordinance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: 122, 106 and 122. And as I‘ve said before in closing, this ordinance was just a part of a massive state policy here of racial segregation and the owner was influenced and compelled by this policy to refuse service to these petitioners at this counter. And the State bears full responsibility for the owner's exclusion where they specifically relied on the ordinance or not because here was a state policy which compelled them to exclude these people from the counter. And we say that for this reason, the Fourteenth Amendment is clearly violated here and the State certainly cannot escape responsibility until such time as it takes affirmative action to change that policy such as has been done in other Northern states by the enactment of civil rights laws. I think the influence of the states designed to set up a segregated society as what is controlling here.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible) exclusion to fill up (Inaudible) City of Birmingham.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Yes they are sir, they are. They're not like the other cases where the policy has been abandoned.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: I didn't quite understand your last point. You -- if the State of Alabama and all its municipality should repeal all statutes and ordinances which requires segregation, you'd still be making your arguments. You'd say the State would have to go further and perhaps pass a suit -- pass a legislation requiring integration before you --

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, I think if those laws were repealed with the understanding and a preamble to the repeal that this was done to do away with state enforced segregation --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: -- we have a different situation. But as long as the State's policy is there on the books, this is the thing which we say influences discrimination in a public place of this kind whether the statute or ordinance specifically says department store or drugstore or whatever the situation maybe that the State's policy is there. And that's the operative consideration which makes the person exclude you. And until the State chose some affirmative change in that policy, that influence is still there, we'd say.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But I didn't get and I still don't quite Mrs. Motley was your reference to affirmative legislation requiring non-segregation.

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: Well, what I have in mind is that here, Alabama has not.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: As there is -- of course there's much to that legislation in the various states, (Voice Overlap)?

turns.name: CONSTANCE BAKER MOTLEY
turns.text: That's right. Alabama in this case has not required equal treatment of Negroes in places of public accommodations as this Court in deciding the civil rights cases assumed that the states would. I remember the civil rights cases, they said, we assumed that the states would protect you against this. Well, this isn't true in Alabama and these other states. The States of course has done just the opposite and they have done it in a massive way so that the whole society is set up on a segregated basis. And this influences the determination of businessman in the State. And we say that the State in order to get away from or escape responsibility for this enforced segregation would have to do something more affirmative than it has now up to this point. It does not protect us against the policy which it has set up.
